column25:
Argue for shift from methodology to conceptualization-  "we believe a fundamental challenge lies not in the structure of methods per se, but how they are understood and presented as singular and objective,"  ... "highlights how the work of designing with a different tempoontology lies not in our methods but in ourselves as ‘Baradian
apparatuses’ [75] which, like the cups do not exist separate from
the phenomenon we wish to understand."..... 

The problems of the scholarly frame -- "Much MTH scholarship is rooted in a tradition of epistemological values that favors objectivity and objectification to establish legitimacy and clarity. This tradition demands clear and precise definitions of contributions"

column1: Mold Sounds: Queering Ecologies in Polyphonic Material Explorations
column4: Riggs, Alexandra Teixeira; Nitsche, Michael; Howell, Noura
column6: Design of an artefact to listen to growth of mould over time
Experiencing historical events through Creeks' perspective
column25:
Being The Creek is not meant as a tool to access certain facts about nonhumans, but it is meant as an invitation for humans to wander and notice nonhumans in their immediate environment from a renewed and embodied perspective. For our participants, this included encounters with some real uncomfortable feelings towards humans and even a sense of loss and hopelessness. Yet, it is through these personally felt experiences, the participants actively questioned humans’ relationship to and responsibility for nonhumans.
Responsibility of designers for more than human-centered worlds, have the power and responsibility to be the “speaking subject” among nonspeaking others.
Point out limitation of conducting work over a single season.
Challenge of what perspective and orientation is represented / engaged with.
participants struggled with the conficting temporalities of natural cycles, the labour of exploration, and intermittent reward.

column4: Fiona Bell, Joshua Coffie, and Mirela Alistar. 2024. Bio-Digital Calendar: Attuning to Nonhuman Temporalities for Multispecies Understanding. In Proceedings of the Eighteenth International Conference on Tangible, Embedded, and Embodied Interaction (TEI '24). Association for Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, Article 38, 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1145/3623509.363338
column6: Build intimate interactions between a user and kombucha through sonification and active listening.
column25:
Keeping the SCOBY alive -- "entanglements and interdependencies between humans and kombucha SCOBY, such as the growth rate of kombucha SCOBY being impacted by the rate at which we refresh the culture or temperature we set in our lab. "
Challenges for other designers foregrounded: "As such, it is easy to fall into the trap of creating temporal binaries when designing bio-digital artifacts, where we try to gain an understanding of the organism by making distinctions between ’human time’ and ’nonhuman time’. However, from a strictly temporal viewpoint, this distinction cannot be made as we are all organisms that constitute a greater “socio-environmental praxis” [1]. In this sense, a bio-digital calendar troubles the notion of human vs nonhuman time, encouraging us to collapse these temporal dualities and unravel binaries between human|nonhuman, subject|object, and nature|culture"

column4: Riel Bessai, Roy Bendor, Ruud Balkenende
column6: Engage with ecosystems, and a range of inhabitants (from human festival goers to plants and natural species)
column24: Indirect reflection from festival goers (what if a bird is nesting the next year, etc?)
column25:
Staging durational temporal engagements raises practical concerns. Building on site, impacted by the rhythm of site (weather, nature, etc.). Nature reclaiming site will shape the future of the festival and what can take place.
"The stage brought awareness to the temporalities of other actors ... but also of the human time pressures that make noticing and caring more difficult. Here is where the challenges – financial, logistical, technical, practical – of applying theory in the real world become visible. While our process had clear limitations – eg. the need to design remotely – making time to pay attention to and care for things outside of the functional scope of the project is already a step in the right direction. Finally, we note that this opening-up means embracing contingency, leaving room for unexpected but symbiotic relationships to emerge across design, especially ones that propose a more caring form of being together."

column1: Designing for Interdependence of Bees, Garden, Designer, and the Changing Season
column4: Yuta Ikeya, Miguel Bruns, Bahareh Barati
column6:
Garden observations and the making of a beehouse mobilizing care for
t he local ecosystem
column24: Attuning to bees, living with ('FA learned to work in the bees’ presence.'), care
column25:
Challenge of season and pace building the bee house: 'FA needed to speed up his making process and occasionally change the order of assembly procedure; he had to complete the assembly of the nesting block (where they nest) before continuing to electronics to make sure that there was a place for bees to live.', 'The FA started using a weather forecast app to predict bee activity and to estimate when he should spend more time on the making. However, when the weather forecast turned out wrong, he had to give up the planning and act according to how the bees were doing.'
Aligning design activities with bees temporalities: 'Eventually, he often ended up working after sunsets while bees are asleep'. Negotiating space: difficulty in rearranging garden once beehouse was built as bees are very sensitive to change. Planning for next year: preparing potential prefered mud source

Unique challenges of designing with plants: grappled with the extensive care essential for nurturing the plants. Their seasonality and environmental sensitivity created uncertainty.
As plants grew from seeds by A1, unpredictability regarding their growth and survival.

column6: Explore first person perspectives through design research with Kombucha Scoby
column25:
Since Kombucha Scoby takes several weeks to grow until its structure is robust enough to be handled (i.e., the biofilm formed isn’t too fragile when removed from the medium), FA got frustrated when she didn’t have enough “material” for completing class projects.
Difference in growing rate of 10 different scoby meant not all were ready for class

column4: Yuta Ikeya, Ron Wakkary, Bahareh Barati
column6: Crafting a design habitat for silk moth caterpillars allowing audience to listen to growth and change over time
column25:
Experience and engagement became less and less special over time for FA. Ethical concerns about hatching insects rhythm and clash with human environment (eg. lack of space).
'Organizing this project according to the designers’ timeline, who may be hastened by tasks and deadlines, we encountered some tensions and conficts between the temporalities of humans and silkworm/moths. For instance, it became difcult for us to leave the progress of the project solely to the pace of the insects. Such time pressure also afected the attention we paid to these insects, degrading the quality of the care routines, especially when their population increased and spatial limitations came into play.'

column4: Jiwei Zhou, Raphael Kim, Zjenja Doubrovski, Joana Martins, Elisa Giaccardi, Elvin Karana
column6: Microbe-embedded artefact challenging human perception of time and gradual change at the microscopic scale.
column25:
In first phase of living with cyanotype, author reports some of the culture died from too much sun exposure. Challenge in caring for species over time?
Challenge in engaging with tacit knowledge from other disciplins 'For instance, when we were developing the cyano unit, we followed a published protocol, in which one of the steps had been omitted . We suspect that this may be due to the fact that its authors may perceive the step to be too trivial to be included in the publication.'

column1: Me, the Hill and My Browser
column6: Attune humans with local nature's temporalities
column24: Felt experience (at home and during the think aloud)
column25:
Use of technology to represent change in time and cycles called attention to specific ones while obfuscating other changes. User noticed 'the design rather than nature'. Conclusion notes: "outline the ’struggles’ with living within two various cultures of time and being torn between noticing the multitude of temporalities."
Potentially challenges around translating out autobiographical designs --- recruiting others would be hard. discussion of bias (but managed) and generalization

column4: Shannon Rodgers, Bernd Ploderer, Kellie Vella, Margot Brereton
column6: Developing a method to study the multifaceted relations between humans and nature and how they are manifested in a particular time and place.

column4: Kellie Vella, Bernd Ploderer, Margot Brereton
column6: Using camera trap to deepen human/nature environment in gardens
column25:
There was a definite learning curve with the camera – it was not super easy to set up and get working correctly. Similarly, participants needed to experiment with diferent camera locations, and be prepared to wait out disappointment over multiple nights and days. Almost all participants expressed confusion at some point as to whether there was nothing to capture or if the camera had malfunctioned.
Most data collection took place one season past Australia’s ’Black Summer’, a period of unusually intense bushfres, which destroyed a record-breaking amount of habitat and native animals.
Participants struggled with the conficting temporalities of natural cycles, the labour of exploration, and intermittent reward. Also link to short time to do research VS longer seasonal time.
Tension between the objectivity of the view and failure of interpretation highlights the limits of using one source of information
